mission.title: Install and Configure Weaviate
mission.description: Weaviate is an open-source vector database that combines vector search with structured filtering, making it ideal for applications like semantic search and recommendation systems. It offers scalability and fault tolerance, suitable for cloud-native environments. Key trade-offs include the need for proper resource allocation and understanding of vector embeddings.

steps.title: Add Weaviate Helm Repository
steps.description:
This step adds the Weaviate Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration, allowing you to install Weaviate using Helm.

```bash

helm repo add weaviate https://weaviate.io/helm-charts

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the Weaviate Helm repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install Weaviate CRDs
steps.description:
This step installs the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) required for Weaviate to function properly in your Kubernetes cluster.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/weaviate/weaviate/main/deploy/k8s/crds/weaviate.io_weaviateclasses.yaml

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/weaviate/weaviate/main/deploy/k8s/crds/weaviate.io_weaviate.yaml

```

> Installs the Weaviate class CRD.
> Installs the Weaviate CRD.

steps.title: Install Weaviate with Helm
steps.description:
This step installs Weaviate using Helm, specifying the namespace and version.

```bash

helm install weaviate weaviate/weaviate --namespace weaviate --create-namespace --version 1.36.0

```

> Installs Weaviate in the 'weaviate' namespace.

steps.title: Create Weaviate Class
steps.description:
This step creates a Weaviate class to store your data.

```bash

kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pods -n weaviate -l app=weaviate -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- curl -X POST 'http://localhost:8080/v1/schema' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"classes":[{"class":"Article","vectorizer":"text2vec-contextionary"}]}''

```

> Creates a class named 'Article'.
commands: kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pods -n weaviate -l app=weaviate -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- curl -X POST 'http://localhost:8080/v1/schema' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"classes":[{"class":"Article","vectorizer":"text2vec-contextionary"}]}''

steps.title: Verify Weaviate is Running
steps.description:
This step checks if the Weaviate pod is running and ready.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n weaviate

```

> You should see the Weaviate pod in a 'Running' state.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Weaviate
uninstall.description:
This step removes Weaviate from your Kubernetes cluster. Be aware that this will delete all data stored in Weaviate.

```bash

helm uninstall weaviate --namespace weaviate

kubectl delete namespace weaviate

```

> Uninstalls Weaviate from the specified namespace.
> Deletes the namespace and all associated resources.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Weaviate
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your data. This step upgrades Weaviate to a new version.

```bash

kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pods -n weaviate -l app=weaviate -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- curl -X GET 'http://localhost:8080/v1/backup' -o backup.json

helm upgrade weaviate weaviate/weaviate --namespace weaviate --version 1.36.0

kubectl get pods -n weaviate

```

> Backs up the current Weaviate data.
> Upgrades Weaviate to the specified version.
> Verify that the Weaviate pod is running the new version.
commands: kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pods -n weaviate -l app=weaviate -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- curl -X GET 'http://localhost:8080/v1/backup' -o backup.json

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Connection refused'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Weaviate pod is not running or not reachable.

**Fix:**
Check the pod status with 'kubectl get pods -n weaviate' and ensure it is in 'Running' state.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Schema validation failed'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The class definition provided does not match the expected schema.

**Fix:**
Review the class definition and ensure it adheres to Weaviate's schema requirements.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient resources'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Kubernetes cluster does not have enough resources allocated for Weaviate.

**Fix:**
Increase the resource limits in the Weaviate deployment or scale your cluster.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to vectorizer'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The vectorizer service is not available or misconfigured.

**Fix:**
Check the vectorizer configuration and ensure it is running correctly.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new vectorization models and improved schema validation.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the old vectorizer API endpoints.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v1.35.0 or earlier, update your API calls to use the new endpoints.

resolution.summary: You should see the Weaviate pod running with the status 'Running' when executing 'kubectl get pods -n weaviate'.
codeSnippets: kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pods -n weaviate -l app=weaviate -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- curl -X POST 'http://localhost:8080/v1/schema' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"classes":[{"class":"Article","vectorizer":"text2vec-contextionary"}]}''

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with sufficient resources and the necessary tools installed.
